initialReview.label: Initial Review Date / Initial Review Reassessment Date
initialReview.description:
On this date, DOCR and Parole Board staff review your case and set or move your Parole Review Date.

This is *not* the date on which the Parole Board decides whether parole will be granted.

paroleReview.label: Parole Board Review Date
paroleReview.description: This is when the Parole Board is scheduled to review your case for possible early release. The Board can grant parole, defer your case, or deny parole. Most cases are handled by paper review instead of an in-person appearance.

goodTime.label: Good Time Release Date
goodTime.description: This is the date you will be released unless A) you are paroled earlier, B) you lose Good Time due to disciplinary write-ups, or C) if you are serving a sentence that does not allow sentence reduction by statute. If you are granted parole, you will stay on parole until your Good Time Release Date.

eightyFivePercent.label: 85% Date
eightyFivePercent.description:
Some sentences require that 85% of your sentence be served in a facility. The 85% date is the earliest that you can be released, even if your Good Time Release Date is earlier.

If no date is shown, the 85% rule does not apply to your sentence — it applies only to certain violent offenses.

finalSentExp.label: Maximum Release Date
finalSentExp.description: The latest date you can be held in custody. Your sentence is legally complete on this date.

sentenceDates.heading: Learn More About Your Sentence
sentenceDates.body:
## What do my dates mean?

Your sentence information shows several important dates. Some are *review dates* (when staff or the Parole Board look at your case). Others are *release dates* (when you might actually go home). Here's what each one means.

###  Review dates

**Initial Review Date.** On this date, DOCR and Parole Board staff review your case and set or move your Parole Review Date. The initial review date is set by the Case Planning Committee and the Transitional Planning Services team.

For sentences three years or longer, the Parole Board will set the Review Date.

**Initial Review Reassessment.** For sentences seven years or longer, the Parole Board may set an Initial Review Reassessment Date between the Initial Review and the Parole Board Review.

The Initial Review Date and the Initial Review Reassessment Date are not the dates on which the Parole Board decides whether parole will be granted.

If you do not see a date here, it may be because your initial review has not been scheduled yet, your sentence does not require one, or you may not be eligible for parole. If your Initial Review already happened, you will not see a date here.

**Parole Review Date.** This is when the Parole Board is scheduled to review your case for possible early release. The Board looks at your programs, conduct, and release plan, and can grant parole, defer your case, or deny parole. Most cases are handled by paper review (known as “Miscellaneous Docket”). TPS and the Parole Board select certain individuals for personal interviews; residents cannot request them.

If the Board defers, your parole order will specify when your next Parole Board Review Date is. If the Board denies parole, you will be required to serve the balance of your current sentence without additional review. If the Board grants parole, a parole date will be set for a date in the future.

Not everyone has a Parole Review Date — it may not be set yet, or you may be ineligible for parole. You will be provided written notices from Transitional Planning and/or Case Planning about your Parole Board Review eligibility and timing and updates if there are any changes.

### Release dates

**Good Time Release Date.** This is when you'll be released if you keep all your Good Time by avoiding disciplinary write-ups and doing your assigned programs. If the Parole Board grants parole, you may be released from custody before your Good Time Release Date. In this case, your parole will conclude on your Good Time Release Date.

**85% Date.** By law, some serious offenses require at least 85% of the sentence to be served in custody. Your Good Time Release Date can’t move earlier than this date. These offenses are determined by the legislature, not by the DOCR.

If you lose Good Time, your Good Time Release Date may get pushed until it is later than your 85% Date. If this happens, you will be held in custody until your Good Time Release Date.

If no date is shown, the 85% rule does not apply to your sentence — it applies only to certain violent offenses.

**Maximum Release Date.** The latest date you can be held in custody. Your sentence is legally complete on this date.

People serving life sentences do not have a Maximum Release Date.

## How does Good Time affect my dates?

In North Dakota, *Good Time* is a credit that reduces your sentence. Your Good Time Release Date is calculated by subtracting 5 days from your maximum release date for every month of your sentence. This calculation happens at the beginning of your prison term and will change if you lose Good Time during your sentence. If your Good Time Release Date changes, **it may take a few days for it to update in the system**. The date you see in this app reflects your most recent GTRD in the system as of yesterday.

If you are granted parole, your GTRD is the day your parole expires. If you lose Good Time during parole, this date will change.

Here are some more details:

- **You get 5 days off for each month of your sentence.** That’s about 60 days off for every year.
- **The date is calculated from day one.** Your Good Time Release Date already includes all your Good Time. It's projected from day one — you start with the full amount applied to your release date, and your job is to keep it.
- **Keeping all your Good Time.** You keep your Good Time by avoiding disciplinary write-ups and doing the programs and work assignments that DOCR recommends.
- **If you lose Good Time.** Disciplinary write-ups can take Good Time away. When that happens, your Good Time Release Date moves later. The amount of Good Time you lose depends on how serious the write-up is. Your case manager can explain how a specific write-up would affect you.
- **Parole revocations.** If you’re on parole and your parole is revoked (taken away), the Parole Board can also take away some or all of your earned Good Time.
- **Earning additional Good Time.** Aside from meritorious credit (which is rare), the only way to earn additional Good Time is by participating in RCST (Resident Crisis Support Team) at certain facilities. Individuals interested in this opportunity should speak with their case manager. Space in RCST is limited.

## Other than Good Time, what credits do I have?

Beyond standard Good Time, other credits can shorten your sentence:

### Jail Time Credit

It is common to receive credit toward your sentence for the time you spent in jail before being moved to DOCR custody — but it must be specified in your Criminal Judgment.

The DOCR cannot grant jail time credit without the court’s order. If you believe you have not been granted correct jail time credit, reach out to your attorney or the clerk of court in the district where you were sentenced.

### Jail Good Time

You may receive credit for good behavior while you were in jail. This is decided by the court, not the DOCR.

### Meritorious Credit

Meritorious credit is very rare. It’s only given for things like saving a life or other acts of heroism. The warden gives this credit, and the DOCR director must approve it. Finishing a GED or treatment does not earn meritorious credit – but it does help your parole case.

## Why can’t some dates move earlier?

You may notice that even if you earn extra credits, your release date stays the same. This is usually because of rules that set the earliest release date:

### The 85% Rule

If you were convicted of certain violent offenses, state law requires you to serve at least 85% of your total sentence before you can be released. You still earn Good Time during an 85% sentence — but your actual release date is whichever is later, your 85% Date or your Good Time Release Date. You can be held past your 85% date if you lose Good Time.

The 85% rule does not apply to sentences imposed when probation is revoked.

**Does it apply to me?** It depends on your specific offense. Your case manager can confirm.

### Minimum Mandatory

Some sentences include a minimum mandatory term — a specific amount of time you must serve before you can be considered for parole. The judge or state law sets this amount.

Not every sentence goes through the Parole Board the same way. **STEP cases** follow a separate release process and don't go to the Parole Board at all. **Minimum mandatory** and **85%** cases can go to the Parole Board, but only after the minimum required portion of the sentence has been served.

Most residents have more than one case and the sentence structure will determine how the sentences are executed by the DOCR.

Your case manager can confirm what type of minimum mandatory you have and how Good Time applies to your specific sentence.

## How does Transitional Housing work?

The Adult Transition Program (ATP) is **not a type of release**. It is a way that some people may qualify to spend the end of their custodial sentence in a transitional facility, also known as a halfway house or a reentry center.

Case managers usually start ATP conversations with eligible residents about 9-12 months before projected release. If you think you’re in that range and haven’t heard from your case manager about your eligibility, submit a kite. Don’t wait to be asked.

### How do I increase my chances of being eligible for ATP?

- **Stay on good behavior**. Disciplinary write-ups in the 6 months before application are the most common reason people get denied.
- **Keep up with treatment and programming.** If treatment is part of your plan, certain programs will need to be completed before consideration.
- **Hold a steady facility job.** ATP is a work program — showing you can hold a job consistently strengthens your application.
- **Complete Career Readiness.** Completing Career Readiness can improve your chances of being approved for ATP.
- **Get your documents started early.** You’ll need a Social Security card and a government photo ID to work legally. If you don’t have both, you may need to order a birth certificate first, which takes time and money. Ask your case manager to help you start this process as soon as possible — earlier than you think.
- **Resolve any holds, warrants, or unpaid restitution.** These can block placement even if everything else looks good.

## How does the STEP Program work?

### What is STEP?

STEP (Sentence and Treatment Early Release Program) is a sentence-reduction option set by the judge at sentencing. It only applies to certain drug and alcohol cases. If your Criminal Judgment includes STEP eligibility and you complete the required treatment, you may be released onto supervision earlier than your Good Time Release Date.

**Important things to know**

- To be STEP-eligible you must have STEP eligible charges that include probation.
- If you have another offense that isn’t STEP-eligible, you can’t be released under STEP — even if one of your cases qualifies.
- You can lose your eligibility through level III write ups or other institutional behaviors committed during your qualifying treatment.
- Your treatment happens inside the facility, while still in DOCR custody. When you successfully finish it, you can be released to probation.
- Treatment staff usually notify your case manager only a week or two before your release plan (where you'll live, how supervision will work) needs to be in place. Be in touch with your case manager well before treatment ends.

## What do I have to do after I’m released?

Your sentence doesn’t always end when you walk out the door. Many residents will have supervision after release.

### Parole Supervision

If you’re granted parole, you’ll be on parole supervision until your Good Time Release Date. You must follow the conditions set by the Parole Board.

### Probation Supervision

Probation is supervision in the community. When probation starts depends on what the court ordered:

- **If probation follows incarceration,** it starts when you're released from prison.
- **If probation follows parole,** it starts after parole is complete.

For some offenses — especially felony sex offenses — probation is longer. It can range from 5 to 10 years, or even lifetime for the most serious offenses. The length of this probation is determined by your Criminal Judgment within sentencing guidelines established by state law.

### Registration Requirements

Certain offenses (especially sex offenses) may require you to register with law enforcement after release.

*Planning ahead:* ask your case manager what supervision will apply to you so you can prepare.

## What happens if…

Here are answers to common questions about how different situations affect your dates:

### I lose Good Time

Your Good Time Release Date will move later. The amount depends on how much Good Time was taken. This change will appear on your profile after the disciplinary process is finished and Legal Records updates your record.

### My parole hearing doesn’t result in release

If parole is denied, the Board will tell you what happens next. You may have to serve the rest of your sentence without further parole consideration, get a new parole review date, or be given a condition to complete before another review

### I complete a program

Completing programs is one of the strongest things you can do to strengthen your parole application. It shows the Board you’re preparing for successful reentry. Most programs don’t automatically change your Good Time Release Date – but they can improve your chances of being granted parole earlier.

### I get a new charge while inside

A new conviction will result in a new sentence. In North Dakota, new sentences often run concurrently with (at the same time as) your existing sentence. That means a new conviction will usually only change your dates if the new sentence is longer than the time remaining on your sentence. But this isn’t always true: a judge can order a new sentence to run consecutively (after your current sentence ends), which would extend your dates.

The DOCR will automatically send you an updated sentence calculation sheet when a new charge occurs. If you believe your dates do not reflect your latest charges, inform your case manager.

### I violate parole and come back

If you're returned to custody for a parole violation, the Parole Board decides how much of your remaining time you'll serve — it could be all of it or part. Legal Records will recalculate your dates after the Board's decision. If you are returning to the custody of the DOCR as a result of parole revocation, the Parole Board will determine if you are eligible to be considered for parole again.

## Who calculates these dates?

The Legal Records Division at the North Dakota Department of Corrections and Rehabilitation calculates these dates. They use your court paperwork and apply North Dakota state law.

### When does the information I see in this app update?

Your dates are pulled into this app from the DOCR system every night. Reasons your dates might change include:

- Something has changed on your record (like a disciplinary action or program completion).
- You have had an Initial Review and your Parole Review Date has been set or updated
- You have had a Parole Review and have been granted parole, deferred, or denied

### What if something looks wrong?

If your dates here don’t match paperwork you’ve received — or you believe there’s an error — submit a kite to the Legal Records Office. If you believe you should have received credit for time served that isn’t on your court paperwork, this needs to be fixed by the court. DOCR cannot add it.

## What do the labels on my Parole Board Review Date mean?

Your case plan shows your Parole Board Review Date with a short label next to the date – like INITIAL, MISC, or INELIGIBLE. This label tells you what kind of review the date is for. There are two kinds of labels.

**Review-type labels** describe what the review actually is:

- **INITIAL** — A review where Parole Board and DOCR staff look at your record to set your Parole Review Date. This applies to residents with more than 3 years left to serve.
- **INELIGIBLE** — You don't have a regular Parole Board review scheduled. This usually means your release pathway is something other than parole – for example, STEP, an 85% sentence, a life sentence without parole, or certain minimum mandatory sentences. If you see INELIGIBLE next to a date, that might be when a review was originally scheduled, before your eligibility was updated.

**Docket-type labels** describe which group of cases your Parole Review is grouped into. These are all Parole Board Reviews.

- **MISC** — The regular monthly docket. Most residents with a scheduled hearing are on this docket.
- **VIOLATOR** — The docket for residents who returned to prison after a parole violation, or whose parole date is being reconsidered by the Board. Violator hearings do not qualify for a personal appearance.
- **PERSONAL** — A hearing where you'll appear in person, rather than by file review. The Board schedules these for specific cases.

If you're not sure what kind of date you're looking at, ask your case manager.

## What if I have more than one sentence?

Many residents are serving more than one sentence at a time. How those sentences fit together affects your dates.

**Concurrent**

Concurrent means the sentences run at the same time. But each sentence is still its own unit — with its own credits, its own jail time, and its own release dates. Concurrent sentences will not always share the same dates. You'll be released only when the latest of them is complete.

**Consecutive**

Consecutive means the sentences run back-to-back. You finish one, then start the next. This makes your total time longer.

The judge decides whether your sentences are concurrent or consecutive. It's written in your court paperwork.

**What you see on this app**: The dates shown here are a summary across all your sentences combined — not the dates of any one sentence on its own. If you want to see how each individual sentence is calculated, refer to your sentence computation sheet, where each Criminal Judgment and count is broken out separately.

**If any of your sentences have special rules:** If one of your sentences includes a minimum mandatory, an 85% requirement, STEP, or other special rules, those rules can affect how DOCR calculates your combined dates — even on sentences that don't have those rules. Your case manager can walk you through how your specific sentences interact.

## Questions about your dates?

Every sentence is unique. Your dates are based on your specific crime, the Criminal Judgment from the court, and your behavior while in custody. If you believe there is an error in your calculation, you may submit a kite to the Legal Records Office.

osuBanner.message: **If you are still in Orientation, you won’t see dates here yet. They will be available after you have been reviewed by the Case Planning Committee.** Tap “Learn More” for information about sentence dates in general.
